title: Littlefield Simulation
keyQuestion: How do we apply operations concepts in a dynamic, realistic simulation?
overview: Littlefield Labs is a hands-on simulation where you manage a medical testing laboratory for about 4 days of real time (representing 104 simulated days). You'll make decisions about machine capacity, inventory management, customer contracts, and pricing while trying to maximize cash at the end. It's like running a real business where you apply everything you've learned: capacity analysis to find bottlenecks, EOQ for inventory decisions, and Factory Physics to understand the WIP-throughput tradeoff.

coreContent.title: Simulation Overview and Objectives
coreContent.content:
Littlefield Laboratories (LL) is a state-of-the-art, highly automated lab that receives medical samples from customers consisting of regional hospitals and clinics. The lab performs a capital-intensive test on each sample and transmits the results back to its customer.

The testing process consists of four steps carried out at three stations named sample preparing, testing and centrifuging. Step 1 at Sample Preparation, Step 2 at Testing, Step 3 at Centrifuge, Step 4 back at Testing.

GOAL: maximize cash position at the end of simulation
coreContent.keyInsight: Real-time decision making under uncertainty requires continuous monitoring and adjustment of operational parameters

coreContent.title: Capacity Management
coreContent.content:
Machine / Station | Average time Step 1, 2, 3 | Average time Step 4 | Total time / station | No. of m/c | Average service time per job | Average Service Rate
Sample Preparation | 5.3 | 0 | 5.3 | 3 | 1.77 | 0.566
Testing | 0.5 | 1.4 | 1.9 | 1 | 1.90 | 0.526
Centrifuge | 1.8 | 0 | 1.8 | 1 | 1.80 | 0.556

Initial # of machines: Sample preparing (3), Testing (1), Centrifuge (1)
Purchase cost: $90,000, $80,000, $100,000 respectively
Order lead time: 5 days for all machines

Need to monitor long-run average utilization when considering machine purchases. With variability in order arrivals and processing times and an emphasis on delivery, you need average utilizations well below 100%.
coreContent.keyInsight: Capacity management requires monitoring both long-run average utilization and short-run peak utilization and queue lengths

coreContent.title: Inventory Management (EOQ Application)
coreContent.content:
Each job requires a test kit which is stored at the materials buffer. A job cannot be processed unless there is a test kit available.
Cost per kit = $600
Fixed ordering cost = $1000
Order lead time = 4 days

Reorder Quantity calculation:
D = 12.2(365) = 4,460 customer orders per year
S = $1000 per order
i = 0.10 per year  
c = $600 per order
EOQ = SQRT(2*1000*4460/(600*0.10)) = 386 orders

Reorder Point calculation:
Leadtime = 4 days (exactly)
D ≈ 12.2 orders per day (on average)
σD ≈ 3.4 orders per day
Average demand over leadtime = 49 orders
St. dev. of demand over leadtime = SQRT(4)*3.4 = 6.7 orders
Reorder point = 49 + 2*6.7 = 62 orders
coreContent.keyInsight: EOQ and reorder point calculations from theory apply directly to real inventory management decisions

coreContent.title: Revenue and Contract Management
coreContent.content:
Revenue based on lead time performance:
Contract 1: $750 revenue, 7 days quoted LT, 14 days max LT
Contract 2: $1000 revenue, 1 day quoted LT, 3 days max LT  
Contract 3: $1250 revenue, 0.5 day quoted LT, 1 day max LT

You collect full revenue if an order is finished within quoted LT. Otherwise revenue decreases linearly from full price to $0 when an order's LT exceeds maximum LT.

Capacity management and marketing strategies must be aligned. A busy factory (high utilization) is efficient but drives up leadtimes – must balance factory productivity with customer needs.
coreContent.keyInsight: Higher revenue contracts require shorter lead times, which demand lower utilization and more capacity investment

coreContent.title: End Game Strategy (Newsvendor Application)
coreContent.content:
The 'End Game': One approach:
Place a large order right before you lose control
Then, set reorder quantity so high that another order will not be placed because you cannot afford it

How big should the final large order be?
Average demand over 50 days = 611 orders
St. dev. of demand over 50 days = SQRT(50)*3.4 = 23.8 orders
Cost of underage = lost contribution = $1250-$600 = $650 per order
Cost of overage = purchase cost = $600 per order
pc = 0.52
Q* = NORM.INV(0.52, 611, 23.8) = 612 orders (cost = $367K)

Another approach: switch to a 'JIT' system to minimize the amount of inventory that you might get 'stuck' with at the end
coreContent.keyInsight: End game requires newsvendor thinking - balancing the cost of too much inventory vs. lost sales

coreContent.title: Variability and Factory Physics
coreContent.content:
Factory physics matters:
A busy factory (high utilization) is efficient but drives up leadtimes – must balance factory productivity with customer needs

Variability in operations hurts more than it helps

Machine purchases based on long-run behavior
Contract management based on short-run behavior

Reaction time matters - need to monitor queues and utilization continuously and react quickly to changes
coreContent.keyInsight: Variability creates the fundamental tension between efficiency (high utilization) and responsiveness (short lead times)

variables.σLT: Standard deviation of demand during lead time = √LT × σdaily

variables.Service_Time: Processing time per job at the station

practiceProblems.question: Given the Littlefield parameters, what is the bottleneck station at the start of the simulation?
practiceProblems.solution: Testing station has the lowest service rate at 0.526 jobs/hour with only 1 machine, making it the initial bottleneck.
practiceProblems.source: Littlefield Debrief Slides After Class Handouts

practiceProblems.question: Calculate the optimal EOQ and reorder point for Littlefield's inventory system.
practiceProblems.solution: EOQ = √(2×1000×4460/(600×0.10)) = 386 orders. ROP = 49 + 2×6.7 = 62 orders (assuming 2 std dev safety stock).
practiceProblems.source: Littlefield Debrief Slides After Class Handouts

practiceProblems.question: What is the end-game optimal order quantity using newsvendor logic?
practiceProblems.source: Littlefield Debrief Slides After Class Handouts

keyInsights: Machine purchases based on long-run behavior
Contract management based on short-run behavior
Capacity management and marketing strategies must be aligned
A busy factory (high utilization) is efficient but drives up leadtimes – must balance factory productivity with customer needs
Variability in operations hurts more than it helps
Managing raw materials requires balancing holding costs and order placement costs – EOQ thinking
End game, and reorder point, are gambles between too much and too little – possible applications of Newsvendor thinking
